did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the YOLOv8n (You Only Look Once version 8 nano) deep learning object detection model to detect and classify skin cancer lesions. The study trained the model on the ISIC 2019 dataset comprising 4,289 images of skin lesions across 9 classes, achieving an overall accuracy of 93.5%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a skin cancer detection system using the YOLOv8n model on the ISIC 2019 dataset. It includes specific sections for Research Methods, Model Training, Model Testing, and Results with quantitative performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score), which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Detection and Classification of Skin Cancer Using YOLOv8n
journal: 2024 11th International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Computer Science and Informatics (EECSI)
brief_summary: This study developed a skin cancer detection and classification system using the YOLOv8n deep learning model to address the limitations of traditional biopsy methods. The model was trained and evaluated on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which comprised 4,289 dermoscopic images across nine distinct skin cancer classes. The dataset was split into training (3,861 images), validation (320 images), and testing (108 images) sets, with images resized to 640x640 pixels. The system was trained for 200 epochs with a batch size of 16 to optimize performance metrics. Experimental results demonstrated high efficacy, achieving an overall accuracy of 93.5%, with precision, recall, and F1-score all exceeding 93%. The findings indicate that YOLOv8n significantly enhances detection accuracy compared to earlier YOLO versions, particularly for classes like Basal Cell Carcinoma and Vascular Lesion which achieved 100% accuracy.

all_task_types.target: Bounding boxes and object class scores for skin cancer lesions
all_task_types.details: The YOLOv8n model predicts bounding boxes (coordinates [x, y], width w, height h) and object class scores for lesions within the image. The system performs object detection by dividing images into grid cells and predicting bounding boxes for each cell.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (9 classes of skin cancer)
all_task_types.details: The model classifies detected lesions into one of 9 discrete classes: Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Actinic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, and Vascular Lesion. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for these classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image collection obtained from the online Kaggle database, comprising images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the YOLOv8n model for 200 epochs with a batch size of 16.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance, consisting of 12 images per class.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

ISIC 2019.notes: The model was trained for 200 epochs with a batch size of 16. Two classes (Basal Cell Carcinoma and Vascular Lesion) achieved perfect detection (100% accuracy/recall). Dermatofibroma had the lowest detection rate with only 10 out of 12 images detected. The training confusion matrix showed performance above 90% for all classes.

architecture_used.main_model: YOLOv8n (You Only Look Once version 8 nano)

architecture_details.backbone: Backbone network using a Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) to extract features
architecture_details.neck: Series of Cross-Layer Connections (CLC) to refine features
architecture_details.head: Predicts bounding boxes, object class scores, and accuracy
architecture_details.block_modifications: C2f replaced with C3; C2f combines all outputs from Bottleneck, while C3 uses only the output from the last Bottleneck
architecture_details.variant: YOLOv8n (nano variant chosen for low CPU performance load and high detection accuracy)

training_details.dataset_split: Training: 3,861 images, Validation: 320 images, Testing: 108 images

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Bounding boxes (coordinates [x, y], width w, height h) and object class scores

preprocessing_techniques: Image annotation with bounding boxes (manual annotation using Roboflow)
Data augmentation via image rotation every 10 degrees
Normalization of confidence scores to a range of 0 to 1

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated every 10 degrees to increase the number of image data and overcome the lack of image data.

conclusions: The YOLOv8n system for detecting and classifying skin cancer lesions into nine classes demonstrated high efficacy, achieving an overall accuracy of 93.5%, precision of 93.5%, recall of 93.7%, and F1-score of 93.5% on the ISIC 2019 dataset.

The YOLOv8n model significantly enhanced detection accuracy and performance compared to previous studies using older YOLO versions (YOLOv1 to YOLOv5), which achieved lower accuracy rates (e.g., 89.1% in prior comparative studies).
Two classes, Basal Cell Carcinoma and Vascular Lesion, achieved perfect detection with 100% accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.
The Dermatofibroma class had the lowest detection performance, with only 10 out of 12 images correctly detected (83.3% accuracy).
The study acknowledges that the number of images per class influences test outcomes, suggesting that balancing dataset quantity for each class is important for consistent performance.
Future work should focus on developing real-time detection systems using cameras and expanding the system into Android and web-based applications for direct patient testing.
important_remarks: The testing dataset is extremely small (108 images total, 12 per class), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the reported 93.5% accuracy.
The paper acknowledges that class imbalance influences test outcomes, noting that balancing dataset quantity for each class is important for consistent performance.
The Dermatofibroma class had the lowest detection performance (83.3% accuracy), with only 10 out of 12 images correctly detected, indicating potential difficulty in distinguishing this class.
Two classes (Basal Cell Carcinoma and Vascular Lesion) achieved perfect 100% accuracy, which may be inflated due to the very small sample size (n=12) per class in the test set.
The study relies on a single public dataset (ISIC 2019) without external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting evidence of real-world clinical applicability.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance differences compared to prior YOLO versions.
Data augmentation was limited to image rotation every 10 degrees, which may not sufficiently address overfitting given the small test set size.
The study is a conference paper with limited scope; future work is proposed for real-time camera-based systems and mobile/web deployment, but no such deployment or clinical trial data is provided.
